summary: Task 6 correctly wires WarmupCoordinator / SettingsMigrationHelper / StartupHousekeeper into SanctumApplication.onCreate behind the packageName guard and adds a working DB-corruption handler in AppModule (rename + sidecar cleanup + AppCorruptionState + history-read log + fresh DB retry). The StartupHousekeeper extraction is a justified deviation from the spec (the spec itself says housekeeping lives in onCreate; pulling it into a @Singleton makes failure branches unit-testable without a full @HiltAndroidApp test graph) and does not change observable behaviour. Two non-blocking concerns: Room.databaseBuilder.build() for the first attempt is constructed outside the try block, and File.renameTo() return value is discarded so the log line can claim a rename that silently failed.

suggestions.suggestion: `buildSanctumDatabase(context)` for the first attempt is invoked BEFORE the try/catch (line 62: `val firstAttempt = buildSanctumDatabase(context)`). Task-6 spec explicitly says `Room.databaseBuilder(...).build()` can throw `SQLiteException`, `IllegalStateException`, or `RuntimeException` and must be wrapped. In practice `build()` is lazy so this is unlikely to fire, but the spec is unambiguous and the deviation leaves one (admittedly rare) exception path uncaught that would otherwise take the recovery branch. Move the first `buildSanctumDatabase(context)` call inside the try block together with the `openHelper.writableDatabase` access, so the catch handler owns the full build+open window the spec enumerates.
suggestions.benefit: Matches the spec verbatim and guarantees that any Room builder exception also triggers the rename-and-retry path instead of escaping to CrashHandler as an uncaught startup failure.

suggestions.suggestion: `dbFile.renameTo(target)` return value is discarded and sidecar deletes (`-journal` / `-wal` / `-shm`) run unconditionally regardless of rename outcome. On Android FAT32/sdcardfs or some vendor filesystems, `renameTo` can silently return `false` (cross-device, locked file, etc.) — and if it does, the subsequent `errorLog.e("history-read", "db open failed — renamed to $corruptName", cause)` logs a rename that did NOT happen, and then the fresh `buildSanctumDatabase(context)` tries to open on top of the still-present corrupt main file, which will throw and propagate to CrashHandler (since the second build is intentionally unwrapped). Capture `val renamed = dbFile.renameTo(target)`, include it in the log line (`"renamed=$renamed"` or take a delete-fallback branch when renamed==false and dbFile.exists()), and consider `dbFile.delete()` as the fallback so the fresh build has a clean slate.
suggestions.benefit: Log accuracy during real corruption incidents (the rename log is the only on-device evidence the user will ever see), and a deterministic path to `buildSanctumDatabase` success even on filesystems where `renameTo` refuses to cross directories.

suggestions.suggestion: `appScope.launch { warmupCoordinator.warmupDefault() }` wraps a NON-suspend, synchronous launcher (`warmupDefault()` immediately returns after calling `scope.launch { ... }` on its own internal scope — see WarmupCoordinator.kt:91). The outer `launch` serves no purpose: it just schedules a second coroutine whose body is a plain function call. Replacing it with a direct `warmupCoordinator.warmupDefault()` call inside the guard block is equivalent, avoids confusing readers into thinking the outer coroutine is the one doing the warmup work, and keeps one less dead Job in the process-lifetime scope. (The `settingsMigrationHelper.migrateIfNeeded()` and `startupHousekeeper.run()` coroutines are genuinely suspend and must stay as they are.)
suggestions.benefit: Removes a layer of coroutine indirection that obscures which scope actually owns the warmup Job (it is WarmupCoordinator's internal scope, not appScope). Makes cancellation semantics clearer when future maintainers read the cold-start sequence.

suggestions.suggestion: `sweepZombieChats failed` is logged with `ErrorLog.e("attachment-save", ...)`. Per `patterns.md § Error logging conventions`, components are a bounded taxonomy: `attachment-save` is one-to-one with attachment write failures; `sweepZombieChats` is a Room-driven history-maintenance operation whose internal row deletions log via `history-write` (ChatRepository.kt:77 docs: `Each removal is logged via ErrorLog.e("history-write", ...)`). Using `attachment-save` for the outer sweep failure conflates two distinct failure modes and makes grep-by-component noisier. Consider using `"history-write"` for the sweep-level catch (keep `attachment-save` for the quick/ and staging/ delete branches which are genuinely attachment-lifecycle work).
suggestions.benefit: Preserves the one-to-one component→failure-mode mapping that patterns.md relies on for on-device log grep-ability.

suggestions.suggestion: `CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Default)` is constructed as a local `appScope` val inside `onCreate` with no reference stored anywhere. Per the task's own notes (`patterns.md` discourages `GlobalScope`) this is intentionally process-lifetime, but the scope is effectively unreachable — nothing can cancel it, and the `Job` it holds will be kept alive forever. Not a leak in practice (process death terminates the JVM), but consider promoting it to a `private val applicationScope = ...` field on `SanctumApplication` so future features (e.g., `onTerminate`, `onLowMemory` cancellation, or tests) have a handle. Also makes it grep-able for anyone hunting for the "app-wide scope" on this codebase.
suggestions.benefit: Single well-named application-scope coroutine handle; easier for Task 10+ code to reuse it rather than minting yet another local scope.

suggestions.suggestion: `testHousekeepingSkippedInCrashProcess` is the only SanctumApplicationTest that covers the new housekeeping wiring, and it only checks `runCount == 0` in the `:crash` branch. The task's TDD Anchor also called for `testQuickPurgeFailureLogsAttachmentSave` and `testStagingOrphanCleanupFailureLogsAttachmentSave` in `SanctumApplicationTest`. Those were migrated to `StartupHousekeeperTest` — which is fine (the extraction justifies it) — but there is no positive-path assertion in SanctumApplicationTest that the main-process branch DOES launch housekeeping. A `testHousekeepingLaunchedInMainProcess` mirror (flipping the process name back to `packageName`, awaiting one tick, asserting `runCount >= 1`) would close the symmetric gap and catch a future edit that accidentally moves the `appScope.launch { startupHousekeeper.run() }` call out of the guard.
suggestions.benefit: Symmetric guard coverage: both branches of the `getProcessName() == packageName` check have explicit tests, so a regression in either direction is caught.

suggestions.suggestion: Class is declared `open` and `run()` is `open suspend fun` purely so `SanctumApplicationTest.RecordingStartupHousekeeper` can subclass it. This leaks test-only mutability into the production API surface. An alternative: keep the class `final`, extract a minimal `interface Housekeeper { suspend fun run() }` (binds `StartupHousekeeper` -> `Housekeeper` in AppModule), and have the test provide a `FakeHousekeeper`. Not a blocker — the current shape is explicitly justified in the KDoc — but worth revisiting when the testing surface stabilises.
suggestions.benefit: Removes `open` from production code, clarifies that the injected abstraction (not the impl) is the contract, and aligns with the project's ChatRepository / ChatDao interface-first pattern.

suggestions.suggestion: `private val workManager by lazy { WorkManager.getInstance(context) }` — the inline comment explains the Robolectric Hilt eager-init motivation well, but the comment lives in `:core-runtime` while the cause (`@HiltAndroidApp` eager graph) lives in `:app`. A cross-reference (`// See AppModule Robolectric note` or `// Workaround for SingletonComponent eager-init in Robolectric tests`) would make this easier to reason about when someone hits the failure from a different entry point and doesn't know the original trigger.
suggestions.benefit: Future maintainers can trace the `by lazy` back to the Hilt graph it protects without grepping for WorkManager across modules.
